coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots suppress proinflammatory T cell responses via autophagy-dependent induction of tolerogenic dendritic cells
coredata.dc:creator: Tomić, Sergej
Janjetović, Kristina
Mihajlović, Dušan
Milenković, Marina
Kravić-Stevović, Tamara
Marković, Zoran

Spitalsky, Zdenko
Micusik, Matej
Vučević, Dragana
Čolić, Miodrag
Trajković, Vladimir
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQD) are atom-thick nanodimensional carbon sheets with excellent physico-chemical and biological properties, making them attractive for application in theranostics. However, their immunoregulatory properties are insufficiently investigated, especially in human primary immune cells. We found that non-toxic doses of GQD inhibit the production of proinflammatory and T helper (Th)1 cytokines, and augment the production of anti-inflammatory and Th2 cytokines by human peripheral blood mononuclear cells. While unable to affect T cells directly, GQD impaired the differentiation and functions of monocyte-derived dendritic cells (DC), lowering their capacity to stimulate T cell proliferation, development of Th1 and Th17 cells, and T-cell mediated cytotoxicity. Additionally, GQD-treated DC potentiated Th2 polarization, and induced suppressive CD4+CD25highFoxp3+ regulatory T cells. After internalization in a dynamin-independent, cholesterol-dependent manner, GQD lowered the production of reactive oxygen species and nuclear translocation of NF-κB in DC. The activity of mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) was reduced by GQD, which correlated with the increase in transcription of autophagy genes and autophagic flux in DC. Genetic suppression of autophagy impaired the pro-tolerogenic effects of GQD on DC. Our results suggest that GQD-triggered autophagy promotes tolerogenic functions in monocyte-derived DC, which could be beneficial in inflammatory T-cell mediated pathologies, but also harmful in GQD-based anti-cancer therapy.

Autophagy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effects of GQD on viability, proliferation and cytokine production of PBMC and T cells. (A, B) PBMC were cultivated in the presence of GQD (50–750 μg/ml) for 24 (A, B) or 48 h (B). The cellular metabolic activity was detected by MTT assay (A), and the percentages of apoptotic and necrotic PBMC were determined by PI staining in hypotonic solution/Triton X and PBS, respectively. (C, D) PBMC were stimulated with PHA in the presence of GQD at the indicated concentrations. After 3 days, cell proliferation was determined by thymidine incorporation assay (CPM – counts per minute), and the cytokine levels in the cell culture supernatants were determined by flow cytometry Th1/Th2 multiplex kit. (E, F) MACS-purified CD3+ T cells were cultivated in the absence or presence of GQD (200 μg/ml), and activated or not by anti-CD3/CD28. After 3 days, cell proliferation was assessed by thymidine incorporation assay (E), and the cytokine levels were measured by ELISA after the treatment with PMA/ionomycin for the last 4 h of culture. (A-F) The results are presented as mean ± SD of three experiments with PBMC from different donors. *p < 0.05 vs. corresponding control; ▲p < 0.05 24 h vs. 48 h, △p < 0.05 200 μg/ml vs. 100 μg/ml GQD).

Mechanism of GQD internalization by DC. (A-E) Immature DC were generated from monocytes with GM-CSF/IL-4 for 4 days, and GQD (200 μg/ml) were added for an additional 24 h. (A) Representative images of DC, taken by phase contrast microscopy at day 5, are shown (black arrows point to accumulated GQD). (B) Representative dot-plots from the flow cytometry analysis of cellular granularity and the summarized data on cellular granularity (% SSlog) from seven independent experiments are shown as mean ± SD. (C) Immature DC were treated with Dynasore (80 μM) or Filipin III (0.8 μg/ml) for 2 h and then with GQD (200 μg/ml) for 24 h. Representative images of May-Grünwald Giemsa-stained cytospin preparations are shown, with the black arrows pointing to blueish agglomerates of GQD within DC. (D) The percentage of GQD+ DC, as determined on May-Grünwald Giemsa-stained slides, is shown as mean ± SD of three independent experiments. (C) Immature DC were treated as in (E), cytospins were prepared and stained with anti-HLA-DR-Alexa 488. Representative confocal images of three independent experiments are shown (See also Supplemental Fig. 2). *p < 0.05 vs. corresponding control (white bars). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

GQD impair differentiation and maturation of DC. (A, B) GM-CSF/IL-4-induced differentiation of DC was carried out in the absence or presence of GQD (200 μg/ml) for 5 days. (A) The percentage of early and late apoptotic (Annexin-V+) and primary necrotic (PI+/Annexin-V-) DC was determined by flow cytometry. The results are shown as mean ± SD of three experiments with different donors. (B) A representative analysis of CD14/CD1a expression on DC differentiated in the presence of GQD for 5 days is shown, and (C) the summarized results collected from six experiments with different donors are presented as mean ± SD. (D) DC were differentiated for 4 days with GM-CSF/IL-4, and then treated or not with GQD (200 μg/ml) for the next 24 h. A representative analysis of CD14/CD1a expression is shown. (E) DC were treated as in (D), and then matured with LPS (500 ng/ml) and IFN-γ (50 μg/ml) for 24 h. The phenotypic analysis of DC was performed by flow cytometry, and the results are shown as mean ± SD of seven experiments with different donors. *p < 0.05 compared to corresponding control, or as indicated.

Effects of GQD on Th-polarizing capacity of DC. (A) GM-CSF/IL-4-differentiated DC were treated or not with GQD (200 μg/ml) for 24 h, followed by LPS/IFN-γ treatment for the next 24 h. The cytokine levels in the cell culture supernatants were analyzed by ELISA. (B-E) The DC from (A) were co-cultivated with MACS-purified allogeneic T cells for 5 days (1:10-1:40 DC-T cell ratio). (B) To test the allostimulatory capacity of DC, T cell proliferation was measured by 3H thymidine incorporation (CPM - counts per minute). (C) T cell cytokine production was measured by ELISA in supernatants of DC/T cell co-cultures upon PMA/ionomycin treatment for the last 4 h (C). (D, E) The intracellular cytokine expression in T cells from DC/T co-cultures was measured by flow cytometry after treatment with PMA/ionomycin/monensin for the last 4 h (A-C, E) The results are presented as mean ± SD of three (E) four (B), or five (A, C) experiments with different DC and T cell donors. *p < 0.05 compared to corresponding control, or as indicated.

GQD reduce the capacity of DC to induce T-cell mediated cytotoxicity. (A) CD83 expression was analyzed by flow cytometry in GM-CSF/IL-4-differentiated DC that were treated or not with GQD (200 μg/ml) for 24 h, and then exposed to necrotic HEp2 cell lysate (nHEp2) and LPS/IFN-γ for the next 24 h (B) DC prepared as in (A) were co-cultivated with autologous T cells for 5 days (1:10-1:40 DC:T cell ratio), and T cell proliferation was measured by 3H thymidine incorporation. The results are presented as mean ± SD of triplicates from a representative of three independent experiments. (C, D) T cells were primed with DC prepared as in (A) (DC:T cell ratio1:20) for 5 days, and then co-cultivated with CFSE-labeled HEp2 cells for 4 h. The number of dead (PI+) HEp2 cells was analyzed by flow cytometry. The representative dot plots are presented in (C), and the summarized data from three experiments with different DC and T cell donors are shown as mean ± SD in (D). *p < 0.05 nHEp2 DC or LPS/IFN-γ nHEP2 DC vs. all other DC.

GQD increase tolerogenic properties of DC. (A, B) The expression of PDL1 and ILT3 was measured by flow cytometry in GM-CSF/IL-4-differentiated DC that were treated or not with GQD (200 μg/ml) for 24 h, followed by LPS/IFN-γ treatment for the next 24 h. (C) CD3+T cells were primed with GQD-treated or control LPS/IFN-γ-matured DC for 3 days, MACS-purified, and then expanded with IL-2 (2 ng/ml) for another 3 days. The percentages of CD4+CD25hiFoxP3+, CD4+CD25hiTGF-β+, or CD4+CD25hiIL-10+ Treg cells were assessed by flow cytometry. D) CFSE-labeled allogeneic responder T cells (Tresp) were stimulated with anti-CD3/CD28 in the presence of different number of T cells primed (Tpr) previously with DC as described in (C), and their proliferation was assessed by flow cytometry. (E) T cells were primed with LPS/IFN-γ-matured control or GQD-treated DC as described in (C), in the presence of isotype control Ab or specific neutralizing anti-ILT3 or anti-PDL1 Abs (all at 2 μg/ml). CD3+ T cells were MACS-purified, followed by Treg expansion with IL-2 (2 ng/ml) for 3 days, and the % of CD4+CD25hiFoxP3+ Treg cells was determined by flow cytometry (see also Supplemental Fig. 4 and Fig. 5). (F) The proliferation of CD4+ and CD4− CFSE-labeled Tresp, in the presence of Tpr from (E) at 2:1 Tresp:Tpr ratio was measured by flow cytometry (see also Supplemental Fig. 7). (B, D-F) The data are presented as mean ± SD of three (C-F) or five (B) experiments with different DC and T cell donors. *p < 0.05 compared to corresponding control, or as indicated.

GQD reduce NF-κB translocation and ROS production in DC. (A, B) GM-CSF/IL-4-differentiated DC were treated or not with GQD (200 μg/ml) for 24 h, and then stimulated with LPS/IFN-γ for 24 h (A) NF-κB translocation was assessed by epi-fluorescence microscopy of cells stained with NF-κB: Alexa488 and DAPI. Representative micrographs are shown, and the summarized data on the relative fluorescence intensity (RFI) of NF-κB in the nucleus divided by that in the cytoplasm of the same cells are presented as mean ± SD of three independent experiments. GQD were detected using excitation 510–560, DM 575, and BA 590. Note that GQD are detectable in the green channel as well. (B) ROS production was measured by flow cytometric analysis of DHR- or DCFDA-stained DC. The representative flow cytometry histograms are shown, and the summarized data from four independent experiments are presented as mean ± SD. *p < 0.05 as indicated. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

GQD induce autophagy in DC. (A) Ultrastructural TEM analysis of GM-CSF/IL-4-differentiated DC that were treated or not with GQD (200 μg/ml) for 24 h. Red arrows point to GQD present in multilamellar vesicles and autophagic vesicles containing cellular material (n-nucleus, m-mitochondria). (B, C) GM-CSF/IL-4-differentiated DC were incubated with GQD, in the presence (B) or absence (B, C) of lysosomal proteolysis inhibitors bafilomycin A1 (BAF; 100 nM) or NH4Cl (20 μM). LC3 conversion (B) or the expression/activation of autophagy proteins (C) was assessed by immunoblotting after 8 h or at the indicating time-points, respectively. The blots from one of two experiments with similar results are shown. (D) GM-CSF/IL-4-differentiated DC were incubated with GQD for 2 h or 8 h, and the mRNA levels of various autophagy regulators and transcription factors were analyzed by real-time RT-PCR. The results are presented as mean ± SD of triplicates from one of two experiments with similar results. *p < 0.05 compared to corresponding control. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

GQD-induced autophagy induced is involved in tolerogenic properties of DC. (A-D) GM-CSF/IL-4-differentiated DC transfected with either control siRNA or ATG5 siRNA were treated with GQD (200 μg/ml) for 24 h, and then stimulated with LPS/IFN-γ for 24 h (A) ATG5 expression was measured by RT-PCR, and autophagic flux was assessed by analyzing LC3 conversion in the presence of bafilomycin A1 (BAF; 100 nM). (B) CD83 and CD86 expression was analyzed by flow cytometry, and the data from a representative of three independent experiments are shown. (C) DC were co-cultured with allogeneic T cells (DC:T cell ratio 1:2), and the percentage of IFN-γ+, IL-4+, and IL-17+ T cells was determined after 5 days by flow cytometry. The data from three experiments with different DC and T cell donors are shown as mean ± SD. (D) The percentage of CD4+CD25hiFoxP3+ T cells after co-culture with DC, followed by expansion with IL-2, was determined by flow cytometry. Representative data from one of two experiments with similar results are shown (see also Supplemental Fig. 9).

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots suppress proinflammatory T cell responses via autophagy-dependent induction of tolerogenic dendritic cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQD) are atom-thick nanodimensional carbon sheets with excellent physico-chemical and biological properties, making them attractive for application in theranostics. However, their immunoregulatory properties are insufficiently investigated, especially in human primary immune cells. We found that non-toxic doses of GQD inhibit the production of proinflammatory and T helper (Th)1 cytokines, and augment the production of anti-inflammatory and Th2 cytokines by human peripheral blood mononuclear cells. While unable to affect T cells directly, GQD impaired the differentiation and functions of monocyte-derived dendritic cells (DC), lowering their capacity to stimulate T cell proliferation, development of Th1 and Th17 cells, and T-cell mediated cytotoxicity. Additionally, GQD-treated DC potentiated Th2 polarization, and induced suppressive CD4+CD25highFoxp3+ regulatory T cells. After internalization in a dynamin-independent, cholesterol-dependent manner, GQD lowered the production of reactive oxygen species and nuclear translocation of NF-κB in DC. The activity of mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) was reduced by GQD, which correlated with the increase in transcription of autophagy genes and autophagic flux in DC. Genetic suppression of autophagy impaired the pro-tolerogenic effects of GQD on DC. Our results suggest that GQD-triggered autophagy promotes tolerogenic functions in monocyte-derived DC, which could be beneficial in inflammatory T-cell mediated pathologies, but also harmful in GQD-based anti-cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQD) are up to 50 nm wide, single-to-several layer-thick oval sheets of graphene, a single layer of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in a honeycomb structure [1]. They possess excellent size-dependent optical properties suitable for biomedical imaging, a better water solubility, biocompatibility, biodegradability, and resistance to photo-bleaching compared to more expensive conventional QD [1–3]. Furthermore, a large surface available for bio-conjugation makes GQD suitable for the preparation of multi-functional drug-delivery systems [4]. GQD can act as both electron donors and electron acceptors, thus displaying a context-dependent pro-oxidant and anti-oxidant activity [5]. Photoexcited GQD generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and kill cancer cells and bacteria [6,7], while in the absence of photoexcitation, GQD protect cells by quenching cytotoxic ROS [6]. Considering that ROS are also key mediators of oxidative stress and redox signaling in immune cells [9], the modulation of ROS by GQD could have a profound impact on immune response. GQD are also able to induce macroautophagy (hereafter autophagy) [8], a process of controlled lysosomal self-digestion of cellular components, which regulates cell survival and death [10] as well as immune response [11]. However, unlike other carbon-based materials [12,13], GQD have not been extensively tested for their immunoregulatory capacity, with only a few available data pointing to opposite immunological effects. While Qin et al. [14] reported that GQD promote a proinflammatory response in THP-1 human monocytic cell line in vitro via induction of ROS and activation of nuclear factor (NF)-κB, we showed that GQD reduce ROS production in vivo and alleviate inflammation-mediated liver damage by suppressing macrophage and T cell functions in mice [15]. Therefore, additional studies on immunological effects of GQD, particularly in primary human cells, are required for their safe application in theranostics, since unwanted stimulation or suppression of the immune response may compromise their efficacy and induce adverse effects.

The regulation of the immune response is best illustrated by the interaction between antigen-presenting cells (APC) and T lymphocytes. Dendritic cells (DC) are the most potent APC able to sense microenvironment and migrate to lymph nodes to activate or tolerize antigen-specific T cells [16]. Fine-tuning of T cell functions by DC enables a tight regulation of the whole immune response, which is why DC-based vaccines are promising candidates for the therapy of cancer, autoimmunity, and transplant rejection [17]. DC-mediated inflammatory response, desirable in cancer therapy, includes phenotypic maturation of DC, production of proinflammatory cytokines such as interleukin (IL)-12, and differentiation of proinflammatory T helper (Th)1 and Th17 cells, as well as cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTL) [18]. In contrast, tolerogenic DC, beneficial in autoimmunity and transplant rejection, express co-inhibitory molecules and secrete anti-inflammatory cytokines, such as IL-10, which enables them to induce anti-inflammatory/immunosuppressive regulatory T cells (Treg) and to potentiate Th1-suppressing Th2 response [19]. It was shown that T cell activation and cytokine production, as well as DC maturation, migration, and the ability to stimulate antigen-specific T cells, all depend on ROS-triggered intracellular signals [20–22]. Furthermore, the functions of both DC and T cells are regulated by autophagy, in a complex interplay with other signaling events [23]. Autophagy was shown to increase antigen presentation and T cell activation by DC [24], but also to limit DC maturation and their ability to secrete proinflammatory cytokines [25–27].

Based on the above data, we hypothesized that GQD intracellular actions, such as modulation of ROS and/or autophagy, may lead to alteration of immunogenic functions of human DC and T cells. Using different in vitro model systems, we show for the first time that GQD suppress proinflammatory T cell responses by potentiating the tolerogenic DC functions via induction of autophagy.

GQD were prepared by electrochemical procedure as previously described [28], using spectroscopic graphite rods both as anode and cathode. A dispersion of NaOH in ethanol (3%) was used as an electrolyte, and the current intensity was set to 20 mA or 50 mA to obtain smaller and larger GQD, respectively. The obtained ethanol solution of GQD was neutralized with HCl, and NaCl was flocculated and separated from the solution by filtration. Following the evaporation of ethanol, the solid was collected and dispersed in acetone to remove acetone-soluble hydrophobic fraction. After acetone removal, GQD were suspended in water and further purified by dialysis (molecular weight cut-off 3500 Da) for several days before dried in a rotary evaporator. GQD were suspended in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) at concentration of 5 mg/ml, sterilized by γ-irradiation (10 kGy), and stored at 4 °C. Endotoxin level in the suspension containing 200 μg/ml of GQD, used in most experiments, was detected by Limulus Amoebocyte Lysate turbidimetric test to be < 0.5 EU/ml, in accordance with the limit provided by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) guidelines [29]. Surface morphology and size of GQD were determined by atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany) equipped with ScanAsyst probes. GQD samples were deposited by spin-coating on freshly cleaved mica and dried in vacuum at 200 °C. The AFM measurements were performed at room temperature in air, using PeakForce QNM imaging mode and Gwyddion software to measure nanoparticle dimensions. Zeta potential was assessed using Zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK) equipped with a 4 mW helium/neon laser (λ = 633 nm) and thermo-electric temperature controller. All measurements were performed at 25 °C, and the value of zeta potential was averaged from five measurements. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a K-Alpha XPS system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA) equipped with a micro-focused, monochromatic Al K-Alpha X-ray source (1486.6 eV). An X-ray beam of 400 mm in size was used at 6 mA × 12 kV, and the spectra were acquired in the constant analyzer energy mode with pass energy of 200 eV for the survey. Narrow regions were collected with pass energy of 50 eV, and charge compensation was achieved with the system flood gun that provides low energy electrons (∼0 eV) and low energy argon ions (20 eV) from a single source. The Avantage software version 5.952 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA) was used for the digital acquisition and data processing. Spectral calibration was determined by using the automated calibration routine and the internal Au, Ag, and Cu standards supplied with the K-Alpha system.

All experiments were approved by the Ethical Board of the Military Medical Academy, University of Defense (Belgrade, Serbia) and carried out in accordance with the Institutional Guidelines. Peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMC) were obtained from buffy coats of healthy volunteers who signed informed consent, using density gradient centrifugation on Lymphocyte Separation Medium 1077 (PAA, Linz Austria). Monocytes and T cells were isolated from PBMC by magnetic-activated cell sorting (MACS) using Monocyte Isolation kit and Pan T cell Isolation kit (Miltenyi Biotec, Bergisch Gladbach, Germany), respectively. The purity of CD14+ monocytes and CD3+ T cells was always higher than 95%, as evaluated by flow cytometry (Cube 6, Sysmex Partec GmbH, Görlitz, Germany). HEp-2 larynx epidermoid carcinoma cells were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (Rockwell, MD). All cells were incubated in complete RPMI-1640 medium containing 10% fetal calf serum, 50 μM 2-mercaptoethanol (all from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO), and 1% streptomycin, gentamicin, and penicillin (ICN Galenika, Serbia) at 37 °C, 90% humidity, and 5% CO2. PBMC (2.5 × 105/well in a 96-well plate) or purified CD3+ T cells (2.5 × 105/well) were stimulated with phytohemagglutinin (PHA) (15 μg/ml; Sigma-Aldrich St. Louis, MO) or plate-bound anti-CD3 (5 μg/ml) and soluble anti-CD28 (1 μg/ml) (both from e-Bioscience, San Diego, CA), respectively. Immature monocyte-derived DC were differentiated from monocytes (2 × 106/well in a 6-well plate) in complete RPMI medium containing 100 ng/ml of recombinant human granulocyte-macrophage colony stimulating factor (GM-CSF) (Novartis, Basel, Switzerland) and 20 ng/ml of human recombinant IL-4 (Roche Diagnostics, Basel, Switzerland) for 5 days, followed by maturation with 500 ng/ml of lipopolysaccharide (LPS) from E.coli 0.111:B4 and 50 μg/ml of interferon (IFN)-γ (both from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO) for 24 h. In some experiments, immature DC were incubated with necrotic lysate (prepared by 10 freezing/thawing cycles) of HEp-2 cells (1:1 DC/HEp-2 cell ratio) for 4 h, and then matured with LPS/IFN-γ. PBMC, T cells, and DC were treated with GQD and/or dynamin inhibitor Dynasore, cholesterol extracting agent Filipin III, or lysosomal proteolysis inhibitors bafilomycin A1 and NH4Cl (all from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO) as described in Results, Figures, and/or Figure legends.

Before co-cultivation experiments, DC were washed three times in complete RPMI medium to prevent the transfer of free GQD. DC (0.125 × 104–1 × 104/well in a 96-well plate) were co-cultivated with MACS-purified allogeneic T cells (1 × 105/well) for 5 days. After the co-cultivation, T cell proliferation was determined, while the supernatants for cytokine measurements were collected after addition of phorbol 12-miristat 13-acetate (PMA, 20 ng/mL) and ionomycin (500 ng/mL) (both from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO) for the last 4 h of incubation. For the flow cytometric detection of intracellular cytokines, co-cultures were treated with PMA/ionomycin and monensin (2 μM; Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO) for the last 4 h of incubation. Alternatively, DC loaded with necrotic lysate of HEp-2 cells were co-cultivated likewise with syngeneic T cells. The cytotoxic activity of MACS-purified T cells was evaluated by their co-incubation with carboxyfluorescein succinimidyl ester (CFSE) (2 μM; Thermo Fischer Scientific, Waltham, MA)-labeled HEp-2 target cells (3 × 104/sample 96 well) at different HEp-2:T cell ratios (1:1-1:4) for 4 h. After incubation, the cells were stained with PI, and HEp-2 cell death was analyzed by flow cytometry based on PI incorporation. In some experiments, CD3+ T cells (5 × 105/well in a 24-well plate) were primed for 3 days with mature DC (1 × 104/well), MACS-purified and expanded with IL-2 (2 ng/mL, R&D Systems, Minneapolis, MN) for an additional 3 days. The primed T cells were analyzed phenotypically, or used in the suppression assay in which different numbers of primed T cells (0.125 × 105–1 × 105/well in a 96-well plate) were co-cultivated with CFSE (2 μM)-labeled allogeneic T cells (1 × 105/well) in the presence of anti-CD3/anti-CD28 for 4 days. The samples were stained with anti-CD4-phycoerythrin (PE) cyanine 5 (Cy5), and the percentage of proliferating CD4+ and CD4− T cells was determined by flow cytometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of cell viability/death, proliferation, and cytokine production
ce:section.ce:para: The viability of PBMC was analyzed by using MTT assay to measure mitochondrial dehydrogenase activity, as described previously [30]. The percentages of necrotic PBMC with disrupted membrane integrity and apoptotic PBMC with a hypodiploid amount of DNA were determined by flow cytometry (Cube 6) after staining with PI/PBS and PI/hypotonic citric/Triton X-100 buffer, respectively [31]. Apoptosis of DC was assessed by Annexin-V/PI staining (R&D Systems, Minneapolis, MN). The proliferation of PBMC and T cells was assessed by 3H-thymidine (1 μCi/well; Amersham BioSciences, Books, UK) pulsing for the last 18 h of culture, and the radioactivity was measured by β-scintillation counter (1219 Rackbeta; LKB-Wallac, Turku, Finland). The proliferation of CFSE-labeled CD4+ and CD4− allogeneic T cells in response to DC-primed T cells was analyzed by flow cytometric measurement of CFSE dilution during cell division [32]. The cytokine concentrations in cell culture supernatants were determined by Th1/Th2 FlowCytomix multiplex kit (Bender MedSystems, Vienna, Austria) or appropriate ELISA kits (R&D Systems, Minneapolis, MN).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flow cytometry analysis of cell phenotype
ce:section.ce:para: The phenotypic analysis of cells was carried out by flow cytometry (Cube 6) using the following antibodies/reagents: IgG1a negative control-biotin (MCA928), antiCD1a-PECy5 (NA1/34HLK), IgG1 negative control-PE (MCA928PE), anti-CD14-Fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) (TUK4), IgG1 negative control-FITC (MCA928F) (all from Serotec, Oxford, UK), anti-human leukocyte antigen (HLA)-DR-biotin (LN3), IgG1a negative control-PECy5 (P.3.6.2.8.1), anti-CCR7-FITC (3D12), anti-CD86-PE (IT2.2), streptavidin-PECy5, anti-CD4-PECy5 (RPA-T4), anti-IL-4-PE (8D4-8), anti-immunoglobulin-like transcript (ILT)3-PE (ZM4.1), anti-programmed death ligand (PDL)1-PE (MIH1), anti-transforming growth factor (TGF)-β-biotin (eBio16TFB), anti-CD25-PE (BC96), anti-FoxP3-FITC (PCH101), anti-CD39-PE (A1), anti-CD3-biotin (UCHT1) (all from eBioscience, San Diego, CA), streptavidin-Alexa 488, anti-mouse IgG-Alexa 488 (Biolegend, San Diego, CA), anti-CD40-FITC (5C3) (BD Pharmingen, San Diego, CA), anti-CD83-FITC (HB15e), anti-IFN-γ-FITC (25723), anti-IL17-PE (41802) anti-IL10-FITC (127107), and anti-CD4-FITC (11830) (R&D Systems, Minneapolis, MN). Surface staining with primary Abs was conducted in PBS/0.1% NaN3/0.5% FBS prior to intracellular staining that was carried out using the flow cytometry fixation and permeabilization kit (R&D Systems, Minneapolis, MN) [32]. Apoptosis of immature DC was assessed by Annexin-V/PI staining (R&D Systems, Minneapolis, MN). Signal overlap between the FL channels was compensated before each analysis using single labeled samples. Non-specific fluorescence was determined by using the appropriate isotype control antibodies and fluorescence minus one (FMO) controls.

The internalization of GQD by DC was assessed by flow cytometry, light microscopy, confocal microscopy, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Flow cytometry was used to assess cellular granularity by monitoring the side scatter (SSC) parameter, the increase of which is frequently used for estimating the intracellular presence of nanoparticles [33]. For confocal microscopy (Zeiss LSM 510, Jena, Germany), the cells were prepared as cytospins and stained with anti-HLA-DR, followed by rabbit anti-mouse IgG-Alexa 488. GQD were visualized with either 543 nm excitation laser and detection filter set at 560–615 nm, or 633 nm excitation laser and longpass detection filter >650 nm, as indicated. Citospin preparations of DC stained with May-Grünwald Giemsa were used for determination of % of cells with GQD in the cytoplasm, by analyzing at least 500 cells per sample. For the TEM analysis, the cells were washed in PBS and fixed with 2% glutaraldehyde/PBS, and then post-fixed in 1% osmium tetroxide (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO). After dehydration in graded alcohols, thin sections were embedded in epoxy medium, sliced on the ultramicrotome, contrasted with lead citrate and uranil acetate (all reagents by Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO), and then analyzed on Morgagni 268D TEM (FEI, Hillsboro, OR).

Epifluorescence microscopy (Olympus BX53, Tokyo, Japan) was carried out for NF-κB localization in DC after staining with anti-NFκB Ab/anti-mouse IgG-Alexa 488 and DAPI. Since GQD were observed in both green and red channels, nuclear translocation of NF-κB was measured as the ratio between the relative fluorescence intensity (RFI) of NF-κB within DAPI+ nucleus and the corresponding area of cytoplasm not containing GQD. At least 200 cells/sample were analyzed using ImageJ software (NIH, Bethesda, MD).

DC were stained during last 15 min of treatment with redox-sensitive dyes dihydrorhodamine 123 (DHR, 80 μM) or 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (H2-DCFDA, 2 μM) (both from Thermo Fischer Scientific, Waltham, MA), and the intensity of FL1 channel fluorescence, corresponding to total ROS levels, was determined by flow cytometry.

Immunoblotting was performed as described previously [15], using antibodies against light chain 3B (LC3), sequestosome 1 (SQSTM1)/p62, Unc-51-like kinase (ULK1), phospho-ULK1 (Ser757), eIF4E-binding protein (4E-BP1), phospho 4E-BP1 (Thr37/46), and actin (all from Cell Signaling Technology, Beverly, MA) as primary antibodies, and peroxidase-conjugated goat anti-rabbit IgG (Jackson IP Laboratories, West Grove, PA) as a secondary antibody. Specific protein bands were visualized using enhanced chemiluminescence reagent (Amersham Pharmacia Biotech, Piscataway, NJ).

Total RNA extraction, reverse transcription, and RT-PCR were performed as described previously [34], using TaqMan primers/probes (all from ThermoFisher Scientific, Waltham, MA) for autophagy-regulated (ATG)3, ATG4B, ATG5, ATG7, ATG10, ATG13, ATG14, activating transcription factor (ATF)4, activating molecule in beclin-1-regulated autophagy 1 (AMBRA1), beclin-1, SH3-domain GRB2-like endophilin B1 (SH3GLB1/Bif-1), BCL2/adenovirus E1B 19 kDa interacting protein 3 (BNIP3), RB1-inducible coiled-coil 1 (RB1CC1/FIP200), forkhead box protein O (FOXO) 1, FOXO3, γ-aminobutyric acid receptor-associated protein (GABARAP), RUN and cysteine-rich domain containing beclin 1-interacting protein (RUBCN), SQSTM1/p62, ULK1, UV radiation resistance-associated gene (UVRAG), and phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase catalytic subunit type 3 (PIK3C3/Vps34). The 18s ribosomal RNA (RN18S) was used as a house-keeping gene. The geometric mean of threshold cycle (Ct) values of RN18S were subtracted from those of target genes to obtain ΔCt, and the relative gene expression was calculated as 2−ΔCt. The results are presented relative to the control value, which was arbitrarily set to 1.

Differentiated immature DC were detached by gentle pipetting, washed twice in OptiMEM medium (Thermo Fischer Scientific, Waltham, MA), and seeded in 24-well plates (1 × 106 cells/well) in OptiMEM medium. DC were then transfected with scrambled small interfering RNA (siRNA) control, or siRNA targeting human beclin-1, ATG5, or SQSTM1/p62 (FlexiTube GeneSolution with 4 preselected siRNAs for each gene; Qiagen, Valencia, CA), using Lipofectamine 2000 Transfection Reagent (Thermo Fischer Scientific, Waltham, MA) according to the manufacturer's instructions. After 8 h, an equal volume of complete RPMI medium was added for the next 16 h, and the transfected DC were treated with GQD and used in assays as described.

The results are presented as mean ± SD values. The differences between treatments were analyzed by T-test (paired or unpaired) or repeated measures analysis of variance (ANOVA) with Tukey's multiple comparison test, using GraphPad Prism software (GraphPad, La Jolla, CA). All tests were two-sided, with the significance level set at p = 0.05.

GQD particles produced by electrochemical exfoliation of graphite using the current intensity of 20 mA and 50 mA were oval-shaped, with the average lateral size of 23.6 ± 7.0 nm and 65.6 ± 8.7 nm, respectively (mean ± SD, n = 100), as demonstrated by AFM analysis (Supplementary Fig. 1). Their average height was approx. 2.0 nm, corresponding to two graphene layers. The zeta potential values, as a measure of particle surface charge, were −9.4 ± 0.8 mV (small GQD) and −9.0 ± 1.5 mV (large GQD) in complete cell culture medium, indicating that both suspensions were electrostatically stabilized. The analysis of XPS C1s spectra of smaller GQD revealed a relatively high sp2/sp3 bond ratio (58.8/27.7 at%), consistent with their high crystallinity and ordered structure. Also, GQD displayed a relatively low content of carbon-oxygen bonds (8.4, 5.1, and 0.0 at% for CO, CO, and OCO bonds, respectively), suggesting a low level of oxygen functionalization of the nanoparticle surface. The biological studies were conducted with smaller GQD (hereafter GQD), while larger GQD were used only in some experiments to examine the effect of GQD size.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD exhibit anti-inflammatory effects in human leukocytes without directly affecting T cells
ce:section.ce:para: Our previous in vivo study [15] showed that GQD suppress Concanavalin (Con)A-induced immune-mediated liver damage. Considerable differences in the organization of immune system exist between mouse and humans, often hampering a direct translation of the results obtained in mouse studies [35]. Therefore, we wondered whether GQD possess similar immunomodulatory properties in human immune cells, especially since a recent study demonstrated a proinflammatory effect of GQD on human THP-1 cell line [14]. We first examined the effects of GQD on human PBMC stimulated with PHA, a model involving the interaction between APC and lymphocytes [36], which is often used for screening immune response in humans [30]. To exclude the possibility that the modulation of PBMC functions is caused by cytotoxicity, we determined initially which dose of GQD is toxic for human PBMC. While no viability loss was observed at the concentrations <300 μg/ml, a decrease in cellular metabolic activity (MTT assay) occurred at higher GQD doses during 72 h of incubation (Fig. 1 A; similar results were obtained after 48 h - data not shown). This was due to necrotic, and to a lesser extent, apoptotic cell death, as demonstrated by cell membrane permeability to propidium iodide (PI) and DNA fragmentation, respectively (Fig. 1B). These results are in line with GQD cytotoxicity data from previous studies [37], and the induction of necrosis at higher doses of GQD is consistent with a recent molecular dynamics simulation model, suggesting that GQD at higher concentrations may change the structure and diffusion properties of the lipid bilayer, and even mechanically damage lipid membrane [38]. To evaluate whether non-toxic concentrations of GQD (<300 μg/ml) could affect the proliferation and cytokine production by PBMC, the cells were stimulated with PHA in the presence of GQD for 3 days. It was found that GQD at 200 μg/ml significantly inhibited the proliferation of PHA-stimulated PBMC (Fig. 1C), as well as the production of proinflammatory (IL-1β, lymphotoxin (LT)-α, IL-6, IL-8) and Th1 cytokines (interferon (IFN)-γ, IL-12), while stimulating anti-inflammatory IL-10 and Th2 cytokines (IL-4, IL-5) (Fig. 1D). Based on the above data, the concentration of 200 μg/ml, as immunomodulatory and non-toxic, was chosen for further experiments. While our results demonstrate the anti-inflammatory effects of electrochemically exfoliated GQD, Qin et al. [14] showed that GQD prepared by hydrothermal oxidation of graphite induced proinflammatory cytokines (tumor necrosis factor (TNF)-α, IL-1β and IL-8) in THP-1 monocytic leukemia cell line at 10 μg/ml, while higher doses (≥50 μg/ml) were toxic. It should be noted, however, that Qin et al. [14] used almost ten-fold lower concentration of PMA than recommended for optimal differentiation of THP-1 cells into macrophages [39], without phenotypically confirming the efficiency of differentiation. Moreover, THP-1 cells were not rested after the treatment with PMA, which was shown critical for their optimal resistance to apoptosis and higher resemblance to monocyte-derived macrophages [40]. Therefore, the apparent discrepancy could be due to different methods used for GQD preparation, but also to different sensitivity and responses of the transformed, compared to primary cells [41]. The anti-inflammatory effect of GQD in human PBMC is consistent with our previous results in mouse model systems, showing GQD-mediated inhibition of IFN-γ production in ConA-stimulated splenocytes and modulation of mitogen-activated protein kinases in Jurkat T cells and J774 macrophages [15]. Since both ConA- and PHA-mediated T cell activation are potentiated in the presence of APC [36,41], we next examined which of the two cell types were the target for GQD immunomodulatory action. While both CD3+ T cells and monocytes isolated from PBMC by negative MACS were able to internalize GQD during 24 h of incubation (Supplementary Fig. 2), the proportion of T cells with visible intracellular GQD agglomerates was lower than 15%, compared to almost 50% of monocytes displaying the intracellular presence of GQD. Moreover, GQD did not significantly affect the proliferation and cytokine (IFN-γ, IL-4, IL-17, and IL-10) production upon anti-CD3/CD28-mediated activation of purified T cells (Fig. 1E, F). Therefore, we hypothesized that GQD exert their immunomodulatory effects predominantly by acting on APC, rather than T cell population.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of GQD internalization by DC
ce:section.ce:para: Functionally different populations of DC, the most potent APC able to activate naïve T cells and regulate their differentiation into effector cells [16,17], are present within PBMC [42]. To study the immunomodulatory action of GQD on DC, we used DC differentiated with GM-CSF/IL-4 and matured with LPS/IFN-γ as a model of Type-1 inflammatory DC [42,43]. Having previously confirmed that monocytes internalize GQD (Supplementary Fig. 2), we next examined if GM-CSF/IL-4-differentiated DC display a similar ability. Light microscopy observation clearly demonstrated the presence of GQD agglomerates in the cytoplasm of immature DC (Fig. 2 A). This correlated with the increased cellular granularity, as observed by monitoring the flow cytometry SSC parameter (Fig. 2B), thus further suggesting the intracellular accumulation of nanoparticles [33]. Previous reports demonstrated that the internalization of GQD occurs via endocytosis [44,45]. Wu et al. [44] showed that endocytosis of GQD 5 nm in size can be blocked by cholesterol-depleting agent Filipin III, but not chlorpromazine, which led them to conclude that the process was caveolae-mediated, rather than clathrin-dependent. By using May-Grünwald Giemsa staining, confocal microscopy, and flow cytometry, we found that Filipin III can also block the internalization of 24 nm GQD by DC (Fig. 2C–E, Supplementary Fig. 3B). On the other hand, the pre-treatment with Dynasore, which inhibits both clathrin- and caveolae-dependent endocytosis by blocking dynamin [46], could not prevent the internalization of these GQD (Fig. 2C–E, Supplementary Fig. 3B). The inhibitory effect of Filipin III on GQD internalization was somewhat potentiated by Dynasore (Fig. 2D, Supplementary Fig. 3B), which could be explained by its ability to additionally reduce labile cholesterol in the plasma membrane in a dynamin-independent manner [47]. The mechanism of nanoparticle internalization by DC greatly depend on their size. For example, we have shown that smaller gold nanoparticles utilize dynamin-independent processes to enter DC, unlike larger nanoparticles which are internalized via dynamin-dependent mechanisms [48]. Therefore, we tested whether size-dependent differences in the mechanism of internalization exist between small GQD (24 nm) and large GQD (66 nm) as well. At the same concentration, large GQD increased DC granularity to a much lesser extent compared to small GQD (Supplementary Figs. 3A and B), suggesting their weaker internalization. Additionally, Dynasore completely blocked the internalization of large GQD, while Filipin III had no significant effect (Supplementary Figs. 3B and C). Collectively, these results suggest that larger GQD enter DC via dynamin-dependent processes, whereas smaller GQD use dynamin-independent and cholesterol-dependent processes, such as those controlled by clathrin/dynamin-independent carriers (CLIC), which are regulated by small GTPase Cdc42 and actin polymerization [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD impair differentiation and maturation of DC
ce:section.ce:para: Proteins involved in dynamin-independent internalization processes are also critically involved in the regulation of DC maturation [49], cytokine secretion [50], migration [51], antigen presentation, and other functions [52]. Therefore, we wondered how GQD upon their internalization modulate differentiation and maturation of DC. Importantly, no increase in early/late apoptotic (Annexin-V+) or necrotic (PI+ Annexin-V-) cells was observed when GQD were present during 5 days of differentiation (Fig. 3 A; similar data were obtained with 66 nm GQD, but not presented for simplicity). The phenotypic analysis showed that, compared to control DC, those differentiated in the presence of GQD expressed significantly lower levels of the DC marker CD1a (Fig. 3B, C, and Supplementary Fig. 3D). The expression of the monocyte marker CD14 on GQD-exposed DC was higher than on control differentiated DC (Fig. 3B, C, and Supplementary Fig. 3D), but still below 10%, suggesting that GQD modulated, rather than completely blocked DC differentiation. CD1a+ DC, unlike CD1a− DC subset, were shown to produce large amounts of IL-12 after maturation, and polarize naïve CD4+ T cells to a proinflammatory Th1 phenotype [53]. Therefore, GQD-mediated down-regulation of CD1a during DC differentiation could indicate their increased anti-inflammatory functions. To examine the effects of GQD on DC maturation, we added GQD at day 4 of differentiation, followed by stimulation at day 5 with LPS/IFN-γ for another 24 h. No alterations in CD14 and CD1a expression were observed before LPS/IFN-γ maturation stimulus (Fig. 3D), confirming that DC differentiation was not affected by the late addition of GQD. However, DC matured in the presence of GQD displayed a significantly lower level of CD40, CD83, CD86, and HLA-DR compared to corresponding LPS/IFN-γ control, while the expression of chemokine receptor CCR7 remained unaltered (Fig. 3E). The effects of large GQD (66 nm) on the differentiation and maturation of DC were similar, but less pronounced (Supplementary Figs. 3D and E), which could be explained by their lower internalization and/or different routes of internalization. Therefore, in addition to impairing DC differentiation, GQD were also able to directly inhibit their maturation. For the sake of simplicity and convenience, for further experiments we used only small GQD (24 nm), added on day 4 of DC differentiation, to monitor the functional consequences of GQD interaction with DC maturation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD impair Th1- and Th17-polarizing, allostimulatory, and anti-tumor capacity of DC
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the observed GQD-mediated cytokine modulation in PHA-stimulated PBMC, and phenotype alterations of mature DC, we investigated if these features correlated with DC functional responses by measuring their cytokine production, allostimulatory capacity, and the ability to polarize the production of cytokines by T cells. Immature DC exposed to GQD did not significantly differ in those functions from corresponding control DC (Fig. 4 A). However, after LPS/IFN-γ-stimulated maturation, GQD-treated DC secreted significantly lower levels of IL-12p70 and IL-23 (Fig. 4A), the major cytokines required for Th1 and Th17 polarization, respectively [53]. The production of anti-inflammatory cytokines IL-10 and TGF-β by GQD-exposed DC were somewhat, but not significantly increased, whereas the levels of immunomodulatory cytokine IL-27 [54] were not modulated by GQD (Fig. 4A). To examine the functions of DC in contact with T cells, allogeneic MACS-purified CD3+T cells were co-incubated with DC at different DC-to-T cell ratios for 5 days. It was found that GQD treatment impaired the capacity of LPS/IFN-γ-matured DC to stimulate the proliferation of allogeneic T cells (Fig. 4B), which is in line with the altered expression of co-stimulators and IL-12p70, the well-known T cell mitogenic factors [18]. Additionally, in comparison with control DC/T cell co-cultures, allogeneic T cells co-incubated with GQD-treated mature DC produced significantly less IFN-γ and IL-17, as well as more IL-4 and IL-10 (Fig. 4C). The intracellular cytokine staining confirmed that significantly lower percentage of IFN-γ+ and IL-17+, and higher percentage of IL-4+ and IL-10+ T cells were present in the co-cultures with GQD-treated compared to control DC, thus confirming a decrease in Th1/Th17 and increase in Th2polarization capacity of DC. The changes were noticeable in both CD4+ and CD4− T cell compartments (Fig. 4D, E). An increased Th2 polarization by GQD-exposed DC can be a direct consequence of their reduced Th1-polarizing ability [55]. As both Th1 and Th17 cells possess anti-tumor potential, unlike Th2 cells which display pro-tumor properties [56,57], we next assessed whether the shift in Th-polarizing capacity of GQD-treated DC towards Th2 might lead to a lower anti-tumor response in vitro. To test this, we used co-cultures of syngeneic T cells and DC that were exposed to necrotic lysate of larynx epidermoid carcinoma Hep-2 cells 4 h before LPS/IFN-γ. In addition to providing tumor antigens, tumor lysate releases endogenous adjuvants that stimulate DC maturation [58], which is why it is being frequently used in anti-tumor DC vaccines [59]. Accordingly, the necrotic Hep-2 lysate, irrespectively of LPS/IFN-γ treatment, stimulated the maturation of DC and proliferation of autologous T cells (Fig. 5 A, B). When DC were treated with GQD in addition to necrotic lysate, a significantly lower maturation and autostimulatory capacity of DC was observed, compared to control Hep-2 lysate-treated DC, irrespective of additional LPS/IFN-γ stimulus (Fig. 5A, B). T cells harvested from 5 day-co-culture with DC were then assessed for their cytotoxicity towards live CFSE-labeled Hep-2 cells, by co-incubating different number of MACS-purified T cells and Hep-2 (Fig. 5C, D). It was observed that T cells primed with DC loaded with necrotic Hep2 lysate induced a higher proportion of dead (PI+) Hep-2 cells, compared to T cells primed with Hep2 lysate-loaded DC treated with GQD, or T cells incubated with DC that were not loaded with tumor cell lysate (Fig. 5C, D). These results confirmed the ability of GQD to diminish the capacity of DC to induce anti-tumor cytotoxicity of tumor antigen-primed syngeneic T cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD induce tolerogenic functions in DC
ce:section.ce:para: Since the tolerogenic capacity of DC correlates with their immature phenotype [19], we next examined if GQD-mediated suppression of DC maturation is associated with the development of tolerogenic properties. One of the key characteristics of tolerogenic DC is the ability to induce Treg [60], which is mainly mediated by co-inhibitory molecules, such as programmed death-ligand (PDL)1 and ILT3 [61,62]. We have found that DC matured with LPS/IFN-γ in the presence of GQD had significantly higher expression of ILT3 and PDL1 compared to corresponding control DC (Fig. 6 A, B). Furthermore, GQD alone or in the presence of LPS-IFN-γ, increased the capacity of DC to induce allogeneic CD4+CD25hiFoxP3+ Treg cells expressing TGF-β, and to a lower extent IL-10 (Fig. 6C, Supplementary Fig. 4 and Fig. 5) as the signature Treg cytokines [63]. Additionally, an increased expression of CD39, which mediates conversion of proinflammatory ATP into anti-inflammatory adenosine [64], was observed in CD4−FoxP3+ and CD4+FoxP3+ T cells after co-incubation with LPS/IFN-γ-matured DC treated with GQD, compared to control DC (Supplementary Fig. 6). To confirm the functional significance of the observed Treg increase, DC-primed T cells were co-cultivated with CFSE-labeled allogeneic responder T cells in the presence of CD3/CD28 stimuli. The proliferation of responder T cells in the presence of T cells primed with GQD-treated mature DC was significantly lower at all responder: primed T cell ratios, compared to corresponding control DC matured in the absence of GQD (Fig. 6D). We have also observed that the expression of FoxP3 (Fig. 6E, Supplementary Fig. 4) and TGF-β (Supplementary Fig. 5) in CD4+CD25hi T cells was completely blocked when T cells were primed with GQD-treated DC in the presence of anti-PDL1 and anti-ILT3 antibodies. Furthermore, the T cells primed in these conditions were unable to suppress the proliferation of responder CFSE-labeled T cells (Fig. 6F, Supplementary Fig. 7). These results confirmed that GQD-treated DC were indeed more tolerogenic, mediating the expansion of suppressive Treg cells via PDL1 and ILT3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD reduce the production of ROS and nuclear translocation of NF-κB
ce:section.ce:para: The above data clearly demonstrate the ability of GQD to induce tolerogenic properties in DC. The activation of the transcription factor NF-κB has been found critical for the development and function of human DC [65], and its inhibition leads to induction of tolerogenic DC [66]. Accordingly, epi-fluorescent microscopy analysis showed that LPS/IFN-γ-induced DC maturation in our experiments was associated with the increased nuclear translocation of NF-κB, which was significantly impaired in DC exposed to GQD during maturation (Fig. 7 A). In contrast, GQD increased nuclear translocation of NF-κB in THP-1 cells [14], and the reasons for this discrepancy could include differences in cell type (DC vs. macrophages, and primary vs. transformed cells), and/or GQD preparation. Since a transient increase in ROS levels has been shown to stimulate NF-κB translocation, phenotypic maturation, antigen presentation, and T cell-activating capacity of DC [20–22], we next used redox-sensitive fluorochromes DHR and DCFDA [67] to measure the effect of GQD on ROS levels in DC. ROS levels did not differ between immature DC and those matured with LPS/IFN-γ (Fig. 7B), probably because mature DC already switched to a low ROS-producing glycolytic metabolism [68]. Nevertheless, both non-stimulated and LPS/IFN-γ-treated DC produced significantly lower ROS levels when exposed to GQD (Fig. 7B). Similar to GQD in the present study, the substances with antioxidant properties were able to impair differentiation of DC and promote their tolerogenic properties, such as reduced IL-12 production upon maturation, increased production of ILT-3 and IL-10, and enhanced capacity to induce differentiation of IL-10-producing T cells [69]. However, some studies indicate that ROS are not indispensable for signaling in DC maturation [20], as well as that tolerogenic DC, due to increased mitochondrial respiration, generate more ROS compared to immature and mature DC [68]. Therefore, while our results demonstrate that GQD-mediated induction of tolerogenic DC is associated with reduced ROS production and NF-κB activation, the involvement of these events in GQD modulation of DC function remains to be directly assessed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQD inhibit mTOR and induce autophagy in DC
ce:section.ce:para: Both NF-κB activation and ROS production are tightly regulated by autophagy and vice versa [70], and we have previously reported that GQD can induce autophagy in cancer cells [8]. Furthermore, mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR), a key regulator of transcription, translation, cell metabolism, and autophagy [71,72], also plays a crucial role in DC differentiation, maturation, and functions [73]. Autophagy is induced both transcriptionally and by mTOR inhibition-mediated activation of ULK1 and subsequent post-translational modifications of ATG proteins [10,72], as schematically depicted in Supplementary Fig. 8. To examine the effect of GQD on autophagy in DC, we employed a multi-faceted approach proposed in recent guidelines [74], consisting of morphological, functional, and intracellular signaling studies. Ultrastructural TEM analysis of immature DC treated at day 4 of differentiation with GQD for 24 h, showed a clear increase in the numbers of intracytoplasmic vesicles (Fig. 8 A, middle), including both double-membrane autophagosomes and single-membrane autophagolysosomes containing GQD and degraded cellular material (Fig. 8A, right). GQD-mediated increase in autophagic turnover (flux) in DC was confirmed by enhanced conversion of microtubule-associated LC3-I to autophagosome-associated LC3-II, whose degradation was blocked with lysosomal proteolysis inhibitors bafilomycin or NH4Cl (Fig. 8B) [74]. The levels of LC3-binding autophagic cargo receptor SQSTM1/p62, while initially increased at 2 h, were decreased after 24 h (Fig. 8C), which is consistent with its selective degradation in autophagolysosomes, and confirms the increase in autophagic flux [75]. The observed induction of autophagy in GQD-treated DC was associated with a decrease in the phosphorylation of mTOR downstream target 4E-BP1 (Fig. 8C). mTOR-dependent phosphorylation of ULK1 at Ser757, which blocks ULK1 activity and subsequent induction of autophagy [72], was also reduced by GQD treatment (Fig. 8C). It therefore appears that GQD induce autophagy in DC by disrupting mTOR-mediated phosphorylation and subsequent inactivation of ULK1. To explore if GQD could activate autophagy transcriptionally, we performed a real-time RT-PCR analysis of 21 genes involved in autophagy induction. It was found that GQD-exposed DC upregulated the mRNA expression of several genes (Fig. 8D) whose products are involved in the initiation of autophagosome formation (RB1CC1, ULK1), autophagosome nucleation (AMBRA1, beclin-1, PIK3C3, UVRAG), autophagosome elongation (ATG4, ATG5, ATG10, GABARAP), and delivery of ubiquitinated proteins to autophagosomes (SQSTM1/p62) [10]. Accordingly, this was associated with the increased levels of mRNA encoding transcription factors ATF4, FOXO1, and FOXO3 (Fig. 8D), which mediate transcriptional activation of ULK1, beclin1, PIK3C3, ATG4, ATG5, GABARAP, and SQSTM1/p62 [10,76], and some of which (FOXO3) are critical for the induction of tolerogenic DC [77]. On the other hand, the mRNA levels of autophagy inhibitor RUBCN [78] were transiently reduced by GQD treatment (Fig. 8D). The observed changes in the transcriptional profile of autophagy-regulating genes are consistent with the autophagy induction by GQD (Supplementary Fig. 8). While the exact mechanisms of GQD-mediated induction of autophagy in DC are still to be defined, there is a possibility that this could be an internalization-dependent event. In line with this, it has been shown that Cdc42, involved in CLIC-mediated dynamin-independent internalization by DC [49], is also involved in the induction of autophagy [79].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Autophagy is involved in GQD-induced generation of tolerogenic DC
ce:section.ce:para: The induction of autophagy by mTOR inhibition has been shown to impair differentiation and maturation of DC [73], as well as to reduce inflammasome-dependent production of proinflammatory cytokines [25–27]. Interestingly, CCR7 expression is maintained after mTOR inhibition, allowing the migration of immature DC and promotion of tolerance [73], which is consistent with our finding that CCR7 expression in GQD-treated DC remained high (Fig. 3E). It has also been demonstrated that SQSTM1/p62-dependent selective autophagy was responsible for NF-κB degradation in mice bone-marrow derived macrophages and their subsequent conversion to a tolerogenic M2 type [80]. These results suggest that the induction of autophagy by GQD could be directly responsible for the observed tolerogenic properties of DC. To test this hypothesis, targeted RNA interference-mediated silencing of ATG5, which is required for autophagosome elongation [10], was performed before GQD treatment. ATG knockdown and its ability to prevent GQD-mediated increase in autophagic flux were confirmed by RT-PCR and immunoblot, respectively (Fig. 9 A). ATG5 silencing diminished the capacity of GQD to inhibit LPS/IFN-γ-induced maturation of DC (Fig. 9B). Moreover, the differences in the ability of GQD-treated and control DC to induce Th1, Th2, and Th17 cells upon maturation were lost upon ATG5 knockdown (Fig. 9C), and ATG5-deficient DC exposed to GQD failed to induce CD4+CD25hiFoxP3 Treg cells (Fig. 9D). GQD-induced maturation block and induction of tolerogenic DC were also prevented by inhibiting autophagy with siRNA against beclin-1 or SQSTM1/p62 (Supplementary Fig. 9). However, these results were somewhat difficult to interpret due to reduced maturation capacity of beclin-1- and SQSTM1/p62-deficient DC, and consequently, their altered Th1 and Th17 polarization capacity (Supplementary Figs. 9B and C). This could be due to a direct involvement of beclin-1 and SQSTM1/p62 in Toll-like receptor (TLR) signaling complexes [81,82], which might have interfered with LPS/IFN-γ-induced maturation process independently of autophagy suppression. Collectively, these data indicate that autophagy induction by GQD was at least partly responsible for their ability to suppress maturation and induce tolerogenic properties of DC.

The present study for the first time demonstrates the ability of GQD to suppress differentiation and maturation of human monocyte-derived DC and to increase their tolerogenic capacity through mechanisms involving both transcriptional and post-transcriptional, mTOR-dependent induction of autophagy. In addition to autophagy-mediated effects, it is possible that GQD might also act independently of autophagy, by interfering with mTOR and/or NF-κB involvement in DC development and functions. While providing novel insights into the mechanisms of GQD immunomodulatory actions, these data indicate their potential usefulness in designing tolerogenic DC vaccines and modulation of DC functions in vivo.

S.T., M.C., and V.T. designed the research, Z.M., B.T.M., Z.S., and M.M.e synthesized and characterized GQD, S.T., D.M., and D.V. performed cell culture experiments, flow cytometry, ELISA, and fluorescent microscopy, K.J. and M.M.b performed RT-PCR and immunoblotting, T.K.S. performed TEM analysis, S.T. and V.T. critically evaluated the results and wrote the manuscript.
